TextENGNew: The final bell rang, and the classroom burst back to life all at once, the students throwing off the stupor that had settled over them during class.
In school life, that bell is, in a certain sense, a sacred thing.
It's nothing more than a sound marking the boundary between class time and everything else—yet everyone waits for it with eager hearts, and the moment it rings, a wave of joy floods through the room.

And if it happens to be the bell ending the last period of the day, that joy swells still larger, bursting loose like a balloon held too tightly—finally, gloriously set free. ...Well, I was among those counting the seconds until it rang.

Sensei Chie called from the lectern, smiling wryly as she made a token effort to settle the explosion of noise. It wasn't a particularly stern reprimand—just a gentle reminder to observe some sense of decorum.
"All right, everyone. Please be careful on your way home, and don't get too carried away. Class rep, please lead the closing greeting."
"Attention, bow!"
At Mion's voice, the entire class stood and bowed together.
""Goodbye~!""
The small classmates bid Sensei Chie farewell in one loud chorus.

On that signal, some bolted out the classroom door, some clustered around Sensei Chie to talk, others grabbed their baseball equipment and made for the exit—each person setting off with their own purpose.

The four of us—Rena, me, Satoko, and Rika-chan—gathered around Mion's desk, all of us with smiles already tugging at our lips. For us club members, the last bell of the day was the signal to begin the battle.
"So, what game are we playing today?"
changeReason: New translation significantly restructures the content, potentially altering the reader's understanding.

TextENGNew: Satoko grinned meaningfully, her enthusiasm for the afternoon's club activities plain to see. I answered her provocation in kind.
"Ooh, Satoko, you already look ready to lose again today?"
"What did you just say~!? Keiichi-san has been getting awfully full of himself lately, hasn't he!?"
When her head was ruffled aggressively, Satoko puffed up, face going bright red, as if to say "don't make fun of me," and turned on me threateningly.
"Enjoy your bravado while it lasts! Once I've caught you in my artistically crafted trap and made you say 'I'm sorry, I'll never defy you again,' you'll understand!"
"Heheh, your imagination certainly is vivid, isn't it?"
"O-Ho-Ho-Ho-Ho!! Whether it's imagination or reality—you'll find out very shortly~!"
Rena and Rika-chan watched our exchange with laughter, the whole familiar scene playing out before the usual pre-club activity skirmish.
"Sorry to ruin the mood, but..."
Mion's expression was genuinely apologetic at having to cut off the fun.
"I have to go to my part-time job today, I'm afraid."
changeReason: New translation is substantially different from original; conveys different content and meaning from the Japanese source text.

TextENGNew: "Oh, really?"
Rena's face fell with disappointment. The rest of us drooped, too, like flowers wilting on the vine.
"Ah, but you can all go ahead and do it without me, you know?"
Seeing us deflate, Mion offered that, and we all exchanged glances.
"Well, but..."

Since I arrived in Hinamizawa, we'd never once held club activities with someone missing—and I was sure it wouldn't be as much fun without every single one of us. I couldn't speak for the others, but for me, the perfect balance of these five people was one of the things that made club activities worth having. Lose even one piece, and the fun would be cut in half, without question.
changeReason: New translation is substantially different from original; conveys different content and meaning from the Japanese source text.

TextENGNew: I couldn't speak for the other four, but for me at least, the exquisite balance maintained by these five was one of the things that made club activities so much fun. Lose even one piece of that, and I was sure the enjoyment would be cut in half.
Besides... holding club activities without the club president, Mion, just felt wrong somehow. The others probably felt the same way.
"So, are we calling it a day? Calling it a day?"
Rena put into words what the rest of us had been searching for.
"Yeah, club just wouldn't be the same without the president there."
"I suppose so.@k@vS19/04/660400107. What a shame, though..."
"It can't be helped.@k@vS19/05/660500086. Mii has her own things to attend to."
changeReason: Old says 'If Mii can't come, she can't come' (dismissive); new says 'Mii has her own things to attend to,' which better matches the Japanese about Mii having her own circumstances.

TextENGNew: Rika-chan patted the visibly crestfallen Satoko on the head and continued with a bright smile.
changeReason: Old omits 'with a bright smile' (にぱーっと笑いながら from the Japanese); new includes this detail.

TextENGNew: "Mii, do your best at work, fight-o~☆"
"Yeah, got it.@k@vS19/03/660300242. Sorry, everyone."
Mion might come across as rough, contrary, and easily embarrassed in her everyday behavior, but she always made sure to apologize properly when an apology was due.
It was precisely because she had this dependable side to her that she could be club president—not just because she was the oldest.
"But in exchange, tomorrow I'm going all out to make up for today.@k@vS19/03/660300244. Hehehe..."
changeReason: Old says 'You'd better be prepared' (threat-focused); new says 'I'm going all out to make up for today,' which is closer to the Japanese 今日の分まで本気出す.

TextENGNew: Mion shed her mask of meekness and twisted her lips into a sly grin. ...Well, tacking on something like that at the end was just like her.
She was right. If today was no good, there was always tomorrow. Tomorrow would come as surely as ever, and it would surely be just as fun as today.
Each of us began packing up to head home, our anticipation for tomorrow's club activities already building.
The dry heat of the season wrapped around my body like a second skin.
On the walk home with just Rena and me, she talked noticeably more than usual to make up for Mion's absence.
She told me about the fierce club battles that had raged before I came to Hinamizawa.

Embarrassing stories about the club members, tales of treasure buried in the garbage heap at the dam construction site, which bus stops were where in Hinamizawa, what day of the week Seventh Mart had sales on what...
None of the topics were particularly weighty, yet each one was packed with information about Hinamizawa that I hadn't known.
I still didn't know enough about Hinamizawa to hold my own in a conversation about it, so this was probably Rena's way of being considerate.

She wanted me to learn about all the things, people, and places connected to Hinamizawa so that I'd come to love this land even more. That feeling came through clearly in every topic Rena effortlessly wove together.
So I listened earnestly to each topic Rena brought up, chiming in with reactions and retorts along the way.
This kind of straightforward kindness from Rena might have felt a little overbearing back when I was living in the city.
But since coming to Hinamizawa, I'd learned to accept even these small kindnesses openly and without hesitation.
——Maybe I'd grown up a little.
Walking beside Rena as she earnestly went on about the bugs that come out this time of year, I let my gaze drop to the gravel road and smiled wryly to myself.
"H-Huh?@k@vS19/02/660200249. Did I say something weird? Something weird?"
"Nah, you didn't say anything wrong."
Not understanding why I was smiling, Rena looked at me curiously. I gave her head a good tousle.
Rena's face flushed pink and she murmured "Hau~" while gazing up at me. When she showed such an easy-to-read reaction like that, it made the head-patting all the more worthwhile.
As we walked along like that, my house came into view.
"Keiichi-kun's house really is big, huh~"
Perhaps because there happened to be a lull in the conversation, Rena had caught sight of my house in the distance and murmured in response.
changeReason: Old says Rena shifted the topic to what Keiichi was looking at; new says Rena caught sight of his house and murmured. Different description of how the house topic came up.

TextENGNew: "Big, maybe, but my room is barely the size of a postage stamp.@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21430. Most of the house is taken up by my dad's work-related rooms."
"Huh~.@k@vS19/02/660200252. Come to think of it, your dad is a painter, isn't he, Keiichi-kun?@k@vS19/02/660200253. What kind of paintings does he do?"
"What kind?@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21450. Hmm~..."
I found myself genuinely lost in thought.
That's right—I had absolutely no idea what kind of paintings my dad made to put food on the table for me and Mom.
He had to be selling reasonably well, given that he'd invite work associates over for parties at the house.
But up until now, I'd never had any real interest in what he was actually painting.
Dad himself never volunteered to show me his work, and I couldn't recall him ever looking pleased when I'd offered to take a look.
At some point, an unspoken rule had quietly taken root deep in my mind: don't touch the subject of Dad's work.
"Could it be... you don't know?"
Watching me ponder for so long, Rena threw me a lifeline.
"Yeah...@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21470. I don't really know what kind of paintings my dad does."
"Really?@k@vS19/02/660200256. Is it a secret? A secret?"
Rena's eyes filled with an apologetic look, as though she was afraid she'd pried into something she shouldn't have.
"No, it's not that I don't want people knowing about my dad's work.@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21490. I just genuinely don't know, that's all."
I scratched my head with a rueful smile.
"I guess I never really gave much thought to what my dad does for work, or how my mom helps out with it..."
We lived under the same roof, and yet I knew surprisingly little about my own parents.
I had a rough answer to the question of why I didn't know, but it was purely my own perspective.@k In other words, it was nothing more than an excuse I'd prepared for myself.
The fact remained that there were all sorts of things about my parents I simply didn't know.
Mom made delicious meals every day. Dad provided a comfortable life for us.
Yet they had never once given me the overbearing feeling of "we're raising you, you know."
Come to think of it, I don't think I'd ever really thought of it as "being raised" either.
Maybe I wasn't grateful enough...
I reflected a little on the way I related to my family. And then, as though she could see right through me, Rena said with a gentle smile:
"That's no good, Keiichi-kun."
Her words were wrapped in Rena's characteristic warm and clear voice, but mixed in—just barely, just the faintest hint—was a note of reprimand.
After that, her tone returned to normal and she continued.
"You only realize how wonderful your family is—when you've lost them."
"...Rena."
For a moment, I caught my breath.@k The 'family' Rena had just mentioned was clearly referring to her own.
At least, that was how it struck me. If my memory was right, this was the first time Rena had ever spoken about her family.
And yet that single remark she'd made about her own family carried an almost oppressive weight.
I wondered if Rena, too, had some kind of burden she was carrying about her family—the same way I didn't know much about my parents.
I was about to open my mouth to ask, but the profile of Rena's face as she walked beside me, gazing into the distance, looked somehow fragile and wistful.
Without thinking, I held my tongue.

Ever since the topic of family had come up, the two of us had been walking in silence, as though the earlier liveliness had been a lie. More than awkward, I sensed with my whole being that pressing the subject simply wasn't wise.

Everyone has things they don't want to talk about, things they don't want others to know. Forcing your way into those things isn't a smart approach. So I didn't try to dig any further into what Rena had said.
Walking together in silence like that for a while, we arrived at the spot where we met every morning before I even realized it.
Normally we'd wave goodbye here and head our separate ways home, but today Rena spoke up.
"Oh, that's right, Keiichi-kun."
"Hm?"
"Can I come pick up the books you said you'd lend me? Lend me?"
"Ah... shoot."
I'd completely forgotten about our promise.
A while back, when we'd been talking about the novels and manga I owned, Rena had taken an interest in a few of them and asked to borrow them sometime.
"Then come by my place to pick them up."
I was pretty sure I'd taken them out of the moving boxes, so they should be on my bookshelf. Even if she came by, she wouldn't have to wait more than a minute at the door.
"Sure, I'll stop by your house then, Keiichi-kun."
Rena nodded and said with a friendly smile.
"I'm home—"
"Hello—"
I opened the front door and ushered Rena inside.
Normally Mom's "welcome back~" would follow right after... or so it should have been, but today, instead, an indescribable stench came wafting out from the kitchen.
changeReason: Old says 'weird smell'; new says 'indescribable stench,' which better matches Japanese 何とも形容し難い臭い. Same event but different intensity.

TextENGNew: "Oh, Keiichi?@k@vS19/00/662600088. Welcome back~"
The face that appeared from the kitchen belonged to... unmistakably my father, wearing a hideously tacky apron covered in little chick prints.
"D-Dad... what are you doing?"
"Can't you tell?@k@vS19/00/662600090. I'm cooking."
"Cooking?! ——Wait!"
Connecting his words with that indescribable odor—no, not smell, odor—I rushed into the kitchen in a panic. And then—
The instant I stepped inside, a stench beyond merely pungent—an outright assault—hit my nose and eyes, and I nearly toppled over and blacked out.
The fact that I somehow managed to stay conscious, bracing myself against the wall, was in itself an achievement worthy of praise.
Through stinging, tear-filled eyes, I stared at the pot sitting on the stove.
"Well? Pretty impressive, right?"
"......My dear father, I'll ask you one more time.@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21620. What. Exactly. Were you doing?"
"I told you, I'm cooking.@k@vS19/00/662600093. Can't you tell?"

"That's cooking?! That is cooking?! If you're telling me that thing bubbling purple smoke and looking like a witch's poison concoction counts as cooking, then I don't know what to believe about the world anymore!!"
Turning to face Dad behind me, I pointed at the pot—which I could no longer bring myself to approach.
Beyond Dad, Rena stood pinching her nose with teary eyes.

...The fact that she hadn't fled was commendable enough for a fellow club member, but what kind of five senses did this man possess that he could stand there calmly tilting his head in confusion in a situation like this?

"'Cooking'?@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21660. You call that cooking?!@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21670. If that thing over there—which looks like a witch brewing poison—counts as cooking, then every housewife in the world is a sorceress!!"
"K-Keiichi-kun... that's going too far...@k@vS19/02/660200264. or maybe not. ...Maybe not."
"......Is it really that bad?"

"The fact that you even have to ask is bad enough!@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21690. It's beyond bad!!@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21700. The only people who could keep their sanity in these conditions are you and a hazmat team!!@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21710. And they'd be wearing gas masks!!!"
"I-It's just, you know, one of those unique aromas you get from a bit of creative cooking...?"
"Have you already forgotten, you third-rate alchemist?!@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21730. Your last so-called 'man's creative cooking' nearly turned our whole family into a tabloid headline!"
"Cooking isn't equivalent exchange—if you're going to try cooking again, at least install extra bathrooms and take out a life insurance policy first!!"
"Y-You d-don't have to go that far..."
"—Then Rena, what's your honest opinion when someone calls that 'cooking'?"
"...Um, well...... it's pretty insul—@k@vS19/02/660200267. I mean, shocking."
The fact that she carefully chose her words and rephrased showed that Rena was still as kind as ever.
"...Anyway, where's Mom? What happened to Mom?"
"Your mother's working overtime tonight.@k@vS19/00/662600097. So your father thought he'd..."
"Then we could just eat out.@k@vS19/01/HR_KEI21780. That'd be easier for you too, wouldn't it?"
"Well... when I suggested eating out, she said, 'Angel Mort again?' in this really cold voice.@k@vS19/00/662600099. If I keep eating out, she might start getting suspicious for no reason..."
Unjustly suspected......? That was dead-on accurate, you old fool.
Lost for words from a mix of anger and exasperation. Still, what were we going to do about dinner... As I racked my brain—
Rena, who had been watching our exchange, timidly made a suggestion.
"...U-Um, Keiichi-kun.@k@vS19/02/660200269. If you'd like, would it be okay if Rena made dinner for everyone? ...Would it?"
changeReason: Old says 'for you'; new says 'for everyone,' matching the Japanese みんなの (everyone's).

TextENGNew: "Huh... are you sure? But—"
I knew firsthand how delicious Rena's cooking was, but it still felt wrong to impose like that—I was about to politely decline when—
"Ohhh?!@k@vS19/00/662600101. OoooOOOOOooo——!!"
"Guhblrgh?!"
Dad shoved me aside with both hands, seized Rena's hands in his, and pressed in on her with an embarrassing display of enthusiasm, practically choking on tears of gratitude.
"Thank you, Rena-chan!@k@vS19/00/662600103. I'm in your debt!@k@vS19/00/662600104. A b-b-beautiful girl's home cooking, banzai!@k@vS19/00/662600105. Banzai!@k@vS19/00/662600106. BANZAIII!"
"D-Dad... you bastard...!"
There was Dad, face beaming as he pumped his fist in the air, and Rena, practically steaming from her head after being called a beautiful girl.
And then there was me, sent flying and sprawled against the wall... If someone had snapped a photo of this scene, it would have looked like nothing but a comedy skit from any angle.
And thus.
Tonight's dinner at the Maebara household was officially decided: a home-cooked meal by beautiful-girl chef Ryuugu Rena (as christened by my clown of a father).
"Oh, and if possible, could you wear a maid outfit...?"
"Will you give it a rest already, you moron!!"
